title: Christ in the Workplace
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Christ in the Workplace - Pastor Tito Lyro - Ephesians 6_5-9.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Addressing the Modern Discomfort with the Slave-Master Passage
subpoints: Paul is addressing the household; by addressing slaves he elevates their status and worth as persons
First-century slavery differed from antebellum chattel slavery in at least four critical ways
Slavery was integral to society — at least a third of the Roman Empire's population was enslaved
The New Testament regulates without condoning slavery, just as it regulates divorce and polygamy without endorsing them

outline.title: Four Ways First-Century Slavery Differed from Modern Assumptions
subpoints: Slavery was universal and across all occupations — medicine was primarily practiced by slaves in the first century
Freedom or liberation was not obviously desirable in the ancient world — the freed slave often struggled to survive
New Testament Christians were a tiny religious group unable to restructure society overnight
Christians realized that whether free or slave, God called them to live in a certain way regardless of circumstance

outline.title: Slavery Is Incompatible with Christianity: The Seed of Abolition
subpoints: There are more slaves in the world today than during the colonial era — mostly under Islamic regimes
The book of Philemon shows Paul sowing the seed of the dissolution of slavery among Christians
Paul treats slaves as thinking, feeling, breathing Christian persons — radical in the Greco-Roman world
The principle of Christian reciprocity between employer and employee is countercultural even today

outline.title: The Lordship of Christ over Work: Do All as unto the Lord
subpoints: Christ frees us not from work but to work more effectively — work was created before the fall
Paul commands working with sincerity of heart as to Christ, not with eye-service as men-pleasers
We are freed from men-pleasing: we work as if the eyes of Christ are upon us because they are
Every lawful vocation can be done for the glory of God — there is dignity in all honest work

outline.title: Wholehearted Service: Christ Frees Us to Work from the Heart
subpoints: Half-hearted service characterizes much of our culture — Paul wages war against it
Work should be viewed as a vocation — a calling from the Lord for that season of life
Young people especially must choose professions thoughtfully as a calling, not just a job
Whatever you do, do it heartily as to the Lord — this applies to school, home, and every lawful occupation

outline.title: The Reward of the Lord and the Accountability of Masters
subpoints: Christians will receive the reward of inheritance even if defrauded by their employers — God does not fail
Every one of us may be placed in circumstances where we are robbed in our labor — the reward is Christ himself
Masters are to do the same things to their servants — reciprocity governs both sides of the relationship
God has no partiality: he holds employers and employees to the same standard

summary:
Preaching from Ephesians 6:5-9, Pastor Tito Lyro opens with a careful contextualization of the slave-master relationship in the first century, noting four crucial differences between ancient slavery and the antebellum chattel slavery familiar to modern Americans. First-century slavery was not racially based, was practiced across all occupations (medicine was primarily a slave profession), and was so widespread that it would barely have registered as an institution to the first Christians. At least a third of the Roman Empire's population was enslaved, and many had sold themselves voluntarily because freedom brought no economic security. The New Testament regulates slavery without condoning it — just as it regulates divorce and polygamy — while the book of Philemon shows Paul quietly planting the seed for its eventual dissolution among Christians.

The heart of the sermon is the application of the Lordship of Christ to the workplace. Paul's command that slaves obey their masters 'as to Christ' and 'not with eye-service as men-pleasers' (verse 5-6) sweeps away the common approach of giving minimum effort when no one is watching. Christ frees us not from work — which was created before the fall — but to work more effectively. Every lawful vocation, whether skilled trades, medicine, homemaking, or poetry, carries equal dignity when done for the glory of God. The sermon challenges the cultural divide between 'spiritual' occupations (ministry, missions) and so-called secular ones, arguing that if God has called you to wash windows, you should be the best window-washer to the glory of God.

Pastor Tito concludes with the promise of verse 8: whatever good anyone does, he will receive the same from the Lord. Even Christians robbed of their rightful wages or fair recognition can rely on the reward of inheritance — and that reward is Christ himself. Masters are held to the same standard: they are to show the same concern for those who work for them, knowing that their own Master in heaven shows no partiality.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Ephesians 6:5-9

If you're able, remain standing. And your Bible is trying to lead you to Ephesians chapter 6. For the reading of God's Word, going to read verses 5 through 9. Ephesians chapter 6, starting in verse 5. This is the Word of our Lord.

*Bondservants, be obedient to those who are your masters according to the flesh, with fear and trembling, in sincerity of heart as to Christ. Not with eye-service as men-pleasers, but as bondservants of Christ, doing the will of God from the heart, with goodwill doing service as to the Lord and not to men, knowing that whatever good anyone does, he will receive the same from the Lord, whether he is a slave or free. And you masters, do the same things to them, giving up threatening, knowing that your own master also is in heaven, and there is no partiality with him.*

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray.

*Father, we pray that you open our eyes to see glorious things concerning you and your Word, for asking in Jesus name. Amen.*

Please be seated.

## Addressing the Modern Discomfort

Our culture chafes under any approval or perceived approval of slavery, even if it's just a perceived approval. Now we read a text like this one and see Paul regulating the relationship of master and slave, and we think Paul is implicitly approving of slavery. That is enough to push people away from the Bible, and they say: I don't want to have anything to do with a book that condones slavery.

Now remember, Paul is addressing the household in this portion of Ephesians. In the first century, slaves were part of the household. By addressing them, Paul is elevating their status and worth as equal to their masters — they are both members of the household.

As we try to get our minds around what this passage is teaching, let me tell you about four ways in which the issue of slavery looked different in first-century Christianity than it does for us today.

### First: Slavery Was Integral to First-Century Society

Slaves served virtually every capacity — from salt miners to doctors. Some scholars say at least a third of the entire population of the Roman Empire was enslaved. Slavery was so much a part of the world of that day that as an institution it would almost have escaped the notice of early Christians. It was so common and so everywhere that it would be very easy not to even see it — it was just there.

### Second: Freedom Was Not an Obvious Good

Freedom or liberation was not, in the first century, the obvious good that it is for us in the modern world. For Americans, slavery is always informed by what? By our knowledge of antebellum Southern slavery — chattel slavery, the atrocities committed in this country, often under the banner of Christianity. But that's not the slavery present in the first century. To be sure, the worst slaves were captured and forced into slavery through wars and other means. But the majority of slaves in the first century sold themselves voluntarily into slavery. It was not racially based.

Interestingly, the English word 'slave' comes from the word 'Slav,' the name of Northern European tribes. So in first-century slavery there was no racial component, there were slaves of all colors, and since they spread over so many occupations there was no sense of solidarity. And legal freedom was not always a positive move. As we see in the parable of the prodigal son, the son in the pigsty concludes he would rather go back and be a hired servant — because under the economy of the time, a hired servant had an even lower status than a household slave.

### Third: New Testament Christians Were a Tiny Group

The New Testament Christians were a tiny religious group living within an all-powerful authority and regime. There was not much they could do at the time to change what was going on.

### Fourth: God's Call Transcends Status

New Testament Christians realized that whether you're free or a slave, God called you to live in a certain way. So they wouldn't bristle under the idea that the Bible tells slaves to behave in a certain way — because if that's where you find yourself, Christ is your Lord and he has a way you are to behave.

## Slavery Is Incompatible with Christianity

Having said all that, a continuation of slavery as an institution is not compatible with Christianity. Slavery and Christianity are not compatible.

The scriptures know to regulate undesirable relationships without condoning them as a permanent ideal. The scriptures regulate divorce. The scriptures regulate polygamy — without condoning them. It's addressing what is: that's where you are, okay, so that's what you need to do, so that things don't get any worse.

I'll quote from Wilhelmus a Brakel on the back of your bulletin: 'The Bible does not approve of or command slavery any more than it approves or commands persecution of Christians. When the author of Hebrews commands his readers by saying, "You joyfully accepted the plundering of your property," that does not mean the Bible supports the plundering of Christians' property or that it commands theft. It only means that if Christians have their property taken through persecution, they should still rejoice because of their heavenly treasure. Similarly, when the Bible tells slaves to be submissive to their masters, it does not mean that the Bible supports or commands slavery, but only that it tells people who are slaves how they should respond.'

Slavery is not grounded in the creation order. It is the result of sin, of the fallen nature of humanity. And on several occasions the New Testament gives us the seed for the dissolution of slavery as an institution — especially in the book of Philemon.

Remember the occasion for the writing of Philemon? A slave named Onesimus escaped his master in Colossae and made his way to Rome where he happened to be put in the same cell as Paul. Paul witnesses to him and Onesimus comes to faith. Paul says: you need to go back to Philemon your master. And then Paul writes the letter of Philemon to go along with Onesimus. And if you read it, this is what you get: Paul writes, 'I'm not telling you what to do, but a good Christian would let this guy go. You owe me a great debt. I'm not asking you to pay the debt. But this man is your brother. Consider him as a brother, not as a slave.'

You can see there where Paul had the influence — he sowed the seed of ending this institution of slavery among Christians. And in 1 Corinthians chapter 7 verse 21, Paul explicitly envisions and endorses the possibility of a slave obtaining freedom: 'Were you called while a slave? Don't be concerned about it. But if you can be made free, rather use it.' So you can see that slavery is not consistent with Christianity.

## Christ Is Lord of the Workplace

In our passage Paul treats slaves as persons — as thinking, feeling, living, breathing, Christian human beings. In the Greco-Roman world of the first century, there was no law concerning rights for slaves. Slaves would have been equivalent to cattle, possessions, things. But Paul speaks to them as persons and instructs them as persons.

Paul sets forth a principle of reciprocity between masters and slaves. Masters must be concerned about those whom they employ, and those who labor must be concerned about the needs of the masters. He expects Christians who are employers and employees to take care of one another and to serve well those with whom they work. That's the principle — employers and employees taking care of one another and serving each other.

Now I really think there is a great problem with the modern view of work. Have you ever seen the bumper sticker: 'I owe, I owe, so off to work I go'? The people who put that on their cars are saying that the main reason for work is to retire debts. Retiring our debts is a good thing, but is that the only or even the main reason to work?

Or perhaps you've heard friends say they can't wait for the weekend — that they live for the weekend, that work is a necessary evil to endure so they can do what they really want. These are not biblical views of work.

## Working as unto the Lord

The first thing we want to see directly from our passage is this: the Christian should do all his work as if he were doing it for the Lord — because he is. Look at verse 5: bondservants, be obedient to those who are your masters according to the flesh with fear and trembling, in sincerity of heart as to Christ.

Paul teaches that Christ has freed us to work more effectively. Christ doesn't free us from work — work is not the result of sin. Work was created before the fall. Even if Adam and Eve had never fallen we would be busy at work, because that's what we were created to do. What Christ has done is freed us to work more effectively.

Christ frees us from men-pleasing and from eye-service. Look again at verses 5 and 6: not with eye-service as men-pleasers, but as bondservants of Christ, doing the will of God from the heart. The tendency of a person who is underpaid and underappreciated is to give a minimalistic effort to the employer. As long as the employer is watching, that person may look like they're working hard. But when the employer is gone, you know what happens.

Paul says: don't work that way. Work as if you are constantly aware that the eyes of Christ are upon you — because that's the truth. We can be selfless and serve Christ in our work.

Christ also frees us to fear him in our work. Look at verse 7-8: with goodwill doing service as to the Lord and not to men, knowing that whatever good anyone does he will receive the same from the Lord whether he is a slave or free. Our accountability is to the Lord, and we can fear him in our work. Colossians 3:23 is even clearer: whatever you do, do it heartily, as to the Lord and not to men.

By saying this, Paul is affirming that all work done for the master can be done with dignity. There's dignity in every lawful employment. It doesn't matter whether you are a member of a learned profession or not. Every lawful vocation can be done for the glory of God.

Anybody know who Booker T. Washington was? A celebrated freed slave in the United States. He said once: no race can prosper until it learns that there is as much dignity in tilling a field as in writing a poem. And vice versa — we live in a society that overemphasizes STEM education so much that we forget about the humanities. There's as much value in one who writes a poem for a living as there is in an engineer.

## Working from the Heart

Paul continues and tells us that the Christian should do all his work from the heart. Not just to finish the day, not just to get a paycheck, but from the heart. Again verse 5: in sincerity of heart as to Christ. Not with eye-service as men-pleasers, but as bondservants of Christ, doing the will of God from the heart.

Have you ever gone to a service establishment where you feel like you're bothering the clerk? Their hearts don't appear to be in it. Paul is saying: don't work that way. Work from the soul. Give yourself in your work. Enjoy your work. Throw yourself into this work that the Lord has given to you.

Look at your work as a vocation — a calling from the Lord for that moment. It may not be what you do for the rest of your life, but for that moment, that's what God is calling you to do and to serve him in that right then.

And this, young people, is why it is so important that you choose your profession carefully. Remember that all work is the result of the Lord's calling — not just the ministry, not just missionary work. If God calls you to be a window washer, be the best window washer you can be. And if God calls you to be a manufacturer, a giver of service, or a professional, seek the Lord's wisdom and guidance so that you can do whatever you do wholeheartedly.

## The Reward of the Lord

Then he says in verse 8 that Christians should realize their work will be rewarded: knowing that whatever good anyone does, he will receive the same from the Lord, whether he is a slave or free.

Paul is speaking to slaves in the Roman world who were obviously not rewarded with great remuneration for their labors. But Paul says: you will be rewarded. In Colossians Paul adds the clause: you will receive the reward of inheritance. Under Greco-Roman law, no slave could inherit anything — it was forbidden. So this clause must have shocked everyone hearing this letter. They were going to be rewarded, because they serve the Master with a capital M — the Lord who is in heaven — and he will not fail to give them their inheritance even if man failed to do so.

Every single one of us, at some time or another, may be put in circumstances where we are robbed in our labor — perhaps through a business relationship, an unfair employer, an employee who steals from us. Paul is saying: no matter how difficult the situation in which you work, no matter if you are being robbed of what is rightfully yours, you will be rewarded. And your reward is going to be Christ himself. And if all that you got was Christ, would you be satisfied with that? If you would not be okay with that, then you need to look at your heart and check: what is it I really believe concerning Christ?

So we see here that God cares about your work and he wants it to be done under the liberty and the rule of Christ — a liberty that frees you to work as unto the Lord, where you're not bound to the standards of this world anymore and you can look for the interests of others as you serve him in your workplace.

*Father, thank you that you are a good God who speaks to every era of life. We pray that you give us a grace to submit to you in this area, that we work for your glory, that we might be able to serve Christ as we bless others around us, for asking in Jesus name. Amen.*
